​@destiny2222 I appreciate your opinion but I also respectfully disagree. For me, shows like "You" shine a light on dangerous behaviours that you would otherwise be unaware of. By way of a very simple example, in Season 1 Joe was able to track Beck down from her social media - I think that served as an important reminder to all that your social media can be used by dangerous people. I think that this was a message a lot of people needed to see / hear. On a deeper level, "You" delved into the psychology of an abusive, love-bombing narcissist and how we, as a society, fail to condemn behaviours and actions committed by a certain demographic in society because they are said demographic. I don't think his behaviours are romanticised - they are shown for what they are time and time again by non-Joe characters
@destiny2222 If someone came away from this show glorifying Joe then I truly worry for their media literacy, they really portrayed it as him getting what he deserved at the end in my opinion

I don’t think You is meant to glamorize or justify Joe’s actions at all, quite the opposite. It’s doesnt patronize behavior like his, even Penn hataes him lol; it holds up a mirror to it. The brilliance of the show is how it lures us into seeing the world through Joe’s distorted lens — and then forces us to confront how easily manipulation and control can be disguised as love.

It’s uncomfortable, yes, but that discomfort is intentional. It’s a tool for reflection, not validation. The fact that people sometimes find themselves empathizing with or rooting for Joe is part of the critique, it exposes how media and society can blur the lines between passion and possession, charm and control.
